era_context: The immediate aftermath of World War I and the 1918 influenza pandemic. The post-war settlement was being implemented through a series of treaties dismantling the German, Austro-Hungarian, Ottoman, and Russian empires. Revolutionary and nationalist movements surged worldwide — Bolshevism consolidated in Russia while fighting a civil war, anti-colonial movements gathered force in India, Ireland, Egypt, and the Middle East, and new states struggled to establish themselves across Central Europe and the former Ottoman lands. The League of Nations launched as humanity's first attempt at collective security governance.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia (sources largely filtered through Soviet or imperial Russian records)
Indigenous peoples of the Americas and Australasia

events.title: League of Nations formally inaugurated
events.description: The League of Nations held its first council meeting on 16 January 1920, the same day the Treaty of Versailles entered into force. The first General Assembly convened on 15 November with 42 member states. It represented the first permanent international organization dedicated to maintaining world peace through collective security, arbitration, and disarmament. However, the United States Senate had rejected the Treaty of Versailles in November 1919 and would do so again in March 1920, meaning the League's most powerful potential member never joined — a structural weakness that would prove fatal.
key_figures: Woodrow Wilson (US President, architect of the League concept)
Léon Bourgeois (first president of the League Council)
Sir Eric Drummond (first Secretary-General of the League)

sources.name: Covenant of the League of Nations (Part I of the Treaty of Versailles, 1919)
F.P. Walters, A History of the League of Nations (1952)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented by official records, diplomatic correspondence, and contemporary press coverage.

events.title: US Senate rejects Treaty of Versailles a second time
events.description: On 19 March 1920, the US Senate failed for the second time to ratify the Treaty of Versailles, falling seven votes short of the required two-thirds majority. A coalition of Republican 'irreconcilables' opposed to any League membership and Democratic senators loyal to Wilson's insistence on ratification without reservations combined to defeat the treaty. This ensured the United States would not join the League of Nations, fundamentally undermining the institution Wilson himself had championed and marking a decisive turn toward American isolationism in the interwar period.
key_figures: Henry Cabot Lodge (Senate Majority Leader, led Republican opposition)

sources.name: US Congressional Record, 66th Congress, 2nd Session (19 March 1920)
John Milton Cooper Jr., Breaking the Heart of the World: Woodrow Wilson and the Fight for the League of Nations (2001)

events.certainty_note: Recorded in official congressional proceedings with exact vote tallies.

events.title: Prohibition takes effect in the United States
events.description: The Eighteenth Amendment to the US Constitution, ratified in January 1919, took effect on 17 January 1920, banning the manufacture, sale, and transportation of intoxicating liquors. The Volstead Act provided enforcement mechanisms. Prohibition reshaped American social life, spawned massive organized crime networks, created a culture of speakeasies and bootlegging, and proved largely unenforceable. It would remain in effect until repeal by the Twenty-First Amendment in 1933.
key_figures: Andrew Volstead (US Representative, authored enforcement legislation)

sources.name: Eighteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution (ratified 16 January 1919, effective 17 January 1920)
Daniel Okrent, Last Call: The Rise and Fall of Prohibition (2010)

events.certainty_note: Constitutional amendment with precise effective date in the text itself.

events.title: Nineteenth Amendment ratified — women's suffrage in the United States
events.description: On 18 August 1920, Tennessee became the 36th state to ratify the Nineteenth Amendment, providing the three-quarters majority needed. The amendment, prohibiting denial of voting rights on the basis of sex, was formally certified on 26 August 1920. This was the culmination of a movement stretching back to the 1848 Seneca Falls Convention. In practice, Black women and other women of colour in the South remained largely disenfranchised through poll taxes, literacy tests, and intimidation — barriers not fully addressed until the Voting Rights Act of 1965.
key_figures: Carrie Chapman Catt (president, National American Woman Suffrage Association)
Harry T. Burn (Tennessee legislator whose decisive vote secured ratification)

sources.name: Nineteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution (certified 26 August 1920)
Elaine Weiss, The Woman's Hour: The Great Fight to Win the Vote (2018)

events.certainty_note: Constitutional amendment with exact ratification dates documented in state and federal records.

events.title: Polish-Soviet War — Battle of Warsaw
events.description: The decisive Battle of Warsaw (12–25 August 1920) saw Polish forces under Józef Piłsudski halt and rout the Red Army's advance into Central Europe. Soviet forces under Mikhail Tukhachevsky had driven to the outskirts of Warsaw, and Bolshevik leaders anticipated the revolution would spread to Germany. The Polish counterattack, sometimes called the 'Miracle on the Vistula,' shattered the Soviet Western Front. The battle is considered one of the most consequential of the 20th century: it preserved Polish independence and halted the westward spread of Bolshevism at a moment when revolution seemed possible across Central Europe.
key_figures: Józef Piłsudski (Polish head of state and commander-in-chief)
Tadeusz Rozwadowski (Polish chief of general staff)

sources.name: Norman Davies, White Eagle, Red Star: The Polish-Soviet War 1919–20 (1972, revised 2003)
Józef Piłsudski, Year 1920 (Rok 1920, 1924)
Mikhail Tukhachevsky, Lecture at the Military Academy on the Vistula Campaign (1923)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented by both Polish and Soviet military records, contemporary dispatches, and diplomatic correspondence.

events.title: Treaty of Sèvres partitions the Ottoman Empire
events.description: Signed on 10 August 1920 between the Allied Powers and the Ottoman government, the Treaty of Sèvres dismembered the Ottoman Empire. It ceded Eastern Thrace and Smyrna to Greece, established an independent Armenia and autonomous Kurdistan, placed the Straits under international control, and formalized the Allied mandate system over the Arab provinces. The treaty was never ratified by the Ottoman parliament and was rendered dead by the Turkish War of Independence led by Mustafa Kemal. It was superseded by the Treaty of Lausanne in 1923, but its mandates framework shaped the modern Middle East.
key_figures: Damat Ferid Pasha (Ottoman Grand Vizier, signatory)

sources.name: Treaty of Sèvres (10 August 1920), text published in British Treaty Series
David Fromkin, A Peace to End All Peace: The Fall of the Ottoman Empire and the Creation of the Modern Middle East (1989)

events.certainty_note: The treaty text survives; its non-ratification and supersession by Lausanne are equally well documented.

events.title: San Remo Conference assigns Middle East mandates
events.description: At the San Remo Conference (19–26 April 1920), the Allied Supreme Council allocated League of Nations mandates over former Ottoman territories. Britain received mandates for Mesopotamia (Iraq) and Palestine (including Transjordan); France received Syria and Lebanon. The conference also incorporated the Balfour Declaration's language supporting a Jewish national home into the Palestine mandate terms. These decisions established the political framework of the modern Middle East, drawing boundaries that persist today and seeding conflicts — particularly over Palestine — that remain unresolved.

sources.name: Minutes of the San Remo Conference, April 1920 (British Foreign Office records)
Margaret MacMillan, Paris 1919: Six Months That Changed the World (2001)

events.certainty_note: Detailed diplomatic records survive from all participating governments.

events.title: Nebi Musa riots in Palestine
events.description: Between 4–7 April 1920, Arab riots broke out in Jerusalem during the Nebi Musa festival, targeting the Jewish community. Five Jews and four Arabs were killed, and over 200 Jews were wounded. The violence was driven by Arab opposition to Zionist immigration and the anticipated incorporation of the Balfour Declaration into the mandate. The British military administration's response was widely seen as inadequate. The riots prompted the Jewish community to organize the Haganah self-defense militia and deepened the communal divide that would define mandatory Palestine.
key_figures: Haj Amin al-Husseini (Arab nationalist leader, later Grand Mufti)
Ze'ev Jabotinsky (Zionist leader, arrested for organizing Jewish self-defense)

sources.name: Palin Commission of Inquiry Report (1920, classified until later release)
Tom Segev, One Palestine, Complete: Jews and Arabs Under the British Mandate (2000)

events.certainty_note: Documented by British military inquiry and contemporary press; casualty figures vary slightly between sources.

events.title: Gandhi launches the Non-Cooperation Movement in India
events.description: In 1920, Mohandas K. Gandhi transformed Indian nationalism by persuading the Indian National Congress (at its Nagpur session in December) to adopt his program of non-violent non-cooperation with British rule. The movement called for boycotting British courts, schools, legislative councils, and foreign cloth, and for returning government honours. Triggered by outrage over the 1919 Jallianwala Bagh massacre and the Khilafat issue (Muslim anger at Allied treatment of the Ottoman Caliphate), the movement united Hindu and Muslim opposition to British rule on an unprecedented scale. It marked the beginning of mass politics in India's independence struggle.
key_figures: Mohandas K. Gandhi (leader, Indian National Congress)
Bal Gangadhar Tilak (nationalist leader, died August 1920)

sources.name: The Collected Works of Mahatma Gandhi, Vols. 17–18 (1920 correspondence and speeches)
Judith Brown, Gandhi's Rise to Power: Indian Politics 1915–1922 (1972)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in Congress session records, Gandhi's writings, and British intelligence reports.

events.title: Kapp Putsch in Germany
events.description: On 13 March 1920, right-wing nationalists led by Wolfgang Kapp and General Walther von Lüttwitz seized Berlin with the support of the Freikorps Marinebrigade Ehrhardt. The Weimar government fled to Stuttgart. The putsch collapsed after five days when a general strike called by trade unions and the SPD paralyzed the country. While the putsch failed, it exposed the German military's refusal to defend the republic (General Hans von Seeckt famously declared 'Reichswehr does not fire on Reichswehr') and the fragility of Weimar democracy. In the Ruhr, a workers' uprising that began as resistance to the putsch was subsequently crushed by the same military that had refused to oppose the coup.
key_figures: Wolfgang Kapp (Prussian civil servant, putsch leader)
Walther von Lüttwitz (General, military leader of the putsch)
Hans von Seeckt (head of the Reichswehr)

sources.name: Johannes Erger, Der Kapp-Lüttwitz-Putsch (1967)
Contemporary German press coverage and government proclamations, March 1920

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in government records, military archives, and contemporary press.

events.title: Treaty of Trianon dismembers the Kingdom of Hungary
events.description: Signed on 4 June 1920 at the Grand Trianon Palace in Versailles, the treaty reduced Hungary to roughly one-third of its pre-war territory and population. Romania received Transylvania, Czechoslovakia gained Upper Hungary (Slovakia), and the Kingdom of Serbs, Croats, and Slovenes received Croatia-Slavonia and parts of the Banat. Approximately 3.3 million ethnic Hungarians found themselves in neighbouring states. The treaty generated enduring Hungarian irredentism that shaped Central European politics throughout the interwar period and remains a sensitive political issue in Hungary today.
key_figures: Miklós Horthy (Regent of Hungary from March 1920)

sources.name: Treaty of Trianon (4 June 1920), published treaty text
Bryan Cartledge, The Will to Survive: A History of Hungary (2011)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text and negotiation records fully preserved.

events.title: Russian Civil War — defeat of the White armies
events.description: By 1920, the Bolsheviks had effectively won the Russian Civil War on most fronts. Denikin's Volunteer Army collapsed in the spring, and command passed to Wrangel, who held Crimea until a final Red Army offensive in November drove him into evacuation. In the east, the remnants of Kolchak's forces had been defeated. Allied intervention forces withdrew. The Bolshevik victory consolidated one-party communist rule over most of the former Russian Empire, though fighting continued in Central Asia and the Caucasus into the early 1920s. The human cost was staggering — the combined death toll from war, famine, and disease during 1918–1921 exceeded that of Russia's WWI losses.

sources.name: W. Bruce Lincoln, Red Victory: A History of the Russian Civil War (1989)
Evan Mawdsley, The Russian Civil War (1987, revised 2007)

events.certainty_note: Documented in extensive Soviet archives, White émigré memoirs, and Allied diplomatic records.

events.title: Turkish War of Independence — Grand National Assembly convenes
events.description: On 23 April 1920, the Grand National Assembly convened in Ankara under the leadership of Mustafa Kemal, establishing a rival government to the Ottoman sultanate in Istanbul, which was under Allied occupation. The Assembly claimed sovereignty over Anatolia and rejected the Treaty of Sèvres. This marked the institutional beginning of the Turkish nationalist movement that would defeat the Greek, French, and Armenian forces occupying Anatolia, abolish the sultanate, and establish the Republic of Turkey in 1923.
key_figures: Mustafa Kemal (Atatürk, president of the Grand National Assembly)

sources.name: Records of the Grand National Assembly of Turkey, 1920 session
Andrew Mango, Atatürk: The Biography of the Founder of Modern Turkey (1999)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in Turkish parliamentary records, diplomatic correspondence, and contemporary press.

events.title: Bloody Sunday and Croke Park massacre in Ireland
events.description: On 21 November 1920, Michael Collins's intelligence operatives assassinated 14 British intelligence agents and informers in Dublin in a coordinated morning operation. That afternoon, British forces (Auxiliary Division and RIC) opened fire on spectators at a Gaelic football match in Croke Park, killing 14 civilians. That evening, three IRA prisoners were killed in Dublin Castle, allegedly while trying to escape. The day marked a dramatic escalation in the Irish War of Independence and demonstrated that the conflict had become an intelligence war that Britain was losing in Dublin.
key_figures: Dick McKee (IRA Dublin Brigade, killed 21 November)

sources.name: British military and police reports, Dublin, November 1920 (National Archives, UK)
Tim Pat Coogan, Michael Collins: A Biography (1990)
James Gleeson, Bloody Sunday (1962)

events.certainty_note: Well documented in British and Irish records, though exact circumstances of some killings remain debated.

events.title: Mexican revolution — Obregón overthrows Carranza
events.description: In April 1920, Álvaro Obregón launched the Plan de Agua Prieta revolt against President Venustiano Carranza, who was attempting to impose a civilian successor rather than allowing Obregón's candidacy. Carranza fled Mexico City and was killed on 21 May in Tlaxcalantongo, Puebla. Obregón won the presidential election in September and took office in December, beginning a period of relative political stabilization. His presidency marked the effective end of the armed phase of the Mexican Revolution and the beginning of post-revolutionary state-building.
key_figures: Venustiano Carranza (outgoing president, killed in flight)
Adolfo de la Huerta (interim president, May–November 1920)
Plutarco Elías Calles (Obregón ally, future president)

sources.name: Plan de Agua Prieta (23 April 1920)
John Womack Jr., Zapata and the Mexican Revolution (1968); Jürgen Buchenau, The Last Caudillo: Alvaro Obregón (2011)

events.certainty_note: Documented in Mexican government archives, contemporary press, and diplomatic correspondence.

events.title: Red Scare and Palmer Raids peak in the United States
events.description: In January 1920, the largest of the Palmer Raids saw Department of Justice agents arrest over 3,000 suspected radicals, anarchists, and communists in 33 cities on a single night. Many were held without warrants; hundreds of foreign-born residents were deported. Attorney General A. Mitchell Palmer justified the campaign as defense against an imminent Bolshevik revolution in America. The raids drew increasing criticism for civil liberties violations, and by summer 1920, when Palmer's predicted May Day revolution failed to materialize, the first Red Scare subsided. The episode demonstrated the vulnerability of civil liberties during periods of political fear.
key_figures: J. Edgar Hoover (head of the Bureau of Investigation's Radical Division)
Louis Post (Assistant Secretary of Labor, blocked many deportations)

sources.name: Report Upon the Illegal Practices of the United States Department of Justice (1920), signed by 12 prominent lawyers
Kenneth Ackerman, Young J. Edgar: Hoover and the Red Scare, 1919–1920 (2007)

events.certainty_note: Documented by Department of Justice records, congressional hearings, and contemporary legal critiques.

events.title: Greco-Turkish War escalates — Greek advance into Anatolia
events.description: Following the Greek occupation of Smyrna (Izmir) in May 1919 with Allied approval, Greek forces launched a major offensive into Anatolia in June 1920, advancing eastward toward Bursa and into Thrace. The campaign was encouraged by the Treaty of Sèvres, which assigned Smyrna and its hinterland to Greek administration. The Greek advance initially succeeded militarily but overextended supply lines and galvanized Turkish nationalist resistance under Mustafa Kemal. The campaign would end in catastrophic Greek defeat and the destruction of Smyrna in 1922.
key_figures: Eleftherios Venizelos (Greek Prime Minister until November 1920)

sources.name: Michael Llewellyn Smith, Ionian Vision: Greece in Asia Minor, 1919–1922 (1973, revised 1998)
British Foreign Office correspondence, 1920 (FO 371 series)

events.certainty_note: Documented in military records, diplomatic correspondence, and contemporary press from multiple countries.

events.title: First commercial radio broadcasts — KDKA and predecessors
events.description: While KDKA's famous broadcast of the Harding-Cox election results on 2 November 1920 is traditionally cited as the birth of commercial radio, several experimental stations were already broadcasting in 1920. Station 8MK (later WWJ) in Detroit began regular broadcasts in August. In the UK, the Marconi Company began experimental broadcasts from Chelmsford. The year 1920 marks the practical beginning of radio as a mass medium, which would transform entertainment, news, politics, and culture over the following decades.

sources.name: Contemporary Westinghouse corporate records and FCC historical files
Erik Barnouw, A Tower in Babel: A History of Broadcasting in the United States, Vol. 1 (1966)

events.certainty_note: The exact 'first' broadcast is debated among historians, but the emergence of regular broadcasting in 1920 is not in dispute.

events.title: Tulsa Race Massacre precursors — racial violence across the US
events.description: The wave of racial violence that began during the Red Summer of 1919 continued into 1920, with lynchings and racial terror across the American South and Midwest. The NAACP documented over 60 lynchings in 1920. The Great Migration of African Americans from the rural South to Northern industrial cities was accelerating, driven by both economic opportunity and flight from violence. Racial covenants, segregation, and white supremacist violence defined the experience of Black Americans. The Duluth, Minnesota lynchings of June 1920, in which three Black circus workers were killed by a mob, shocked Northern audiences who had considered such violence a Southern phenomenon.

sources.name: NAACP annual reports and The Crisis magazine, 1920
Cameron McWhirter, Red Summer: The Summer of 1919 and the Awakening of Black America (2011)

events.certainty_note: Documented in NAACP records, contemporary Black press, and recent historiographic recovery efforts.

events.title: Soviet republics established in the Caucasus
events.description: In April 1920, the Red Army invaded Azerbaijan and established the Azerbaijan Soviet Socialist Republic, ending the two-year-old Azerbaijan Democratic Republic. In November–December, Soviet forces similarly conquered Armenia, which had been devastated by war with Turkey and famine. The brief independence of the Caucasian republics (1918–1920/21) ended as the Bolsheviks reasserted Russian imperial control under communist ideology. Georgia would fall in February 1921. These conquests determined the political fate of the region for seven decades.
key_figures: Grigory Ordzhonikidze (Soviet commander in the Caucasus)
Sergo Kirov (Bolshevik organizer in the Caucasus)

sources.name: Firuz Kazemzadeh, The Struggle for Transcaucasia, 1917–1921 (1951)
Soviet military orders and Bolshevik party correspondence, 1920 (published in various document collections)

events.certainty_note: Documented in Soviet archives, Caucasian government records, and British diplomatic correspondence.

events.title: Haiyuan earthquake in China kills over 200,000
events.description: On 16 December 1920, a magnitude 7.8 earthquake struck Haiyuan County in the Ningxia region of northern China, triggering massive landslides in the loess plateau terrain. The death toll is estimated at 200,000–270,000, making it one of the deadliest earthquakes in recorded history. Entire villages were buried under landslides. The disaster occurred in a remote, impoverished region during a period of political chaos (warlord era), and relief efforts were minimal. The event received relatively little international attention compared to its scale.

sources.name: Chinese seismological records and local gazetteers (fangzhi)
Deng Qidong et al., research on the 1920 Haiyuan earthquake (published in various Chinese geological journals)

events.certainty_note: Seismologically confirmed; death toll estimates vary between 200,000 and 270,000 due to poor census data in the region at the time.

events.title: Warren G. Harding elected US President on a 'return to normalcy' platform
events.description: On 2 November 1920, Republican Warren G. Harding won a landslide victory over Democrat James M. Cox, receiving 60.3% of the popular vote — the largest margin of victory to that point in American history. It was also the first presidential election in which women could vote nationally. Harding's campaign for a 'return to normalcy' captured the national mood of exhaustion with Wilsonian idealism, war, and reform. The election signalled the end of the Progressive Era and the beginning of a decade of Republican pro-business governance, isolationism, and cultural conservatism.

sources.name: Official election returns, US Federal Election Commission predecessor records
John W. Dean, Warren G. Harding (2004, American Presidents Series)

events.certainty_note: Official election records with exact vote tallies.

events.title: Congress of the Peoples of the East in Baku
events.region: Baku, Azerbaijan SSR / Global anti-colonial movements
events.description: From 1–8 September 1920, the Bolsheviks convened the Congress of the Peoples of the East in Baku, attended by nearly 1,900 delegates from across the Middle East, Central Asia, and South Asia. Grigory Zinoviev called for a 'holy war' against British imperialism. The congress attempted to link communist revolution with anti-colonial movements in the colonized world. While it produced few concrete organizational results, it signaled the Bolshevik strategy of supporting anti-imperial movements globally and foreshadowed the Cold War competition for influence in the developing world.
key_figures: Grigory Zinoviev (president of the Communist International)
Enver Pasha (former Ottoman war minister, attended)

sources.name: Proceedings of the Congress of the Peoples of the East, Baku, September 1920 (published by the Communist International)
Stephen White, 'Communism and the East: The Baku Congress, 1920,' Slavic Review (1974)

events.certainty_note: Full proceedings published; attendance figures and delegate backgrounds documented.

disconfirming_evidence: Several commonly cited claims for 1920 require qualification. (1) The 'Miracle on the Vistula' narrative (1920-005) has been debated: Polish nationalist historiography emphasizes Piłsudski's personal genius, while some historians (notably Norman Davies) argue the victory resulted from a combination of factors including Soviet logistical overextension, poor coordination between Tukhachevsky and Budyonny's cavalry, and intercepted Soviet communications — not a single miraculous counterstroke. The Soviets blamed their own commanders; the Poles credited divine intervention. (2) KDKA's claim as the 'first commercial radio station' (1920-018) is disputed by several other stations (8MK/WWJ Detroit, 2XG New York, and others) that made similar claims. The priority depends on how 'commercial broadcasting' is defined. (3) The significance of the Palmer Raids (1920-016) is sometimes overstated in progressive narratives as a fundamental threat to American democracy, while revisionist scholars note that most raids targeted genuinely revolutionary organizations and that the legal system ultimately checked the worst abuses (e.g., Louis Post canceling warrants). However, the civil liberties violations were real and documented. (4) Gandhi's Non-Cooperation Movement (1920-009) is sometimes presented as having universal Indian support, but many moderate nationalists, landlords, and business interests opposed it, and it was called off in February 1922 after the Chauri Chaura violence — suggesting the movement's control was more fragile than hagiographic accounts admit.
historiographic_note: 1920 is richly documented through government archives, diplomatic records, press coverage, and personal correspondence from virtually all regions involved. However, significant biases shape the record: European and American sources dominate; colonial records reflect administrative rather than indigenous perspectives; Soviet-era archives were selectively accessible until 1991; and the experiences of ordinary people — particularly in China's warlord period, colonial Africa, and the colonized world generally — are vastly underrepresented compared to elite diplomatic and military narratives. The post-WWI settlement period has been extensively studied by Western historians, but coverage of simultaneous events in East Asia, Southeast Asia, Latin America beyond Mexico, and sub-Saharan Africa remains comparatively thin in English-language scholarship.

graph_edges.note: The League of Nations was established by Part I of the Treaty of Versailles
Polish victory at Warsaw led to the Treaty of Riga (March 1921) setting the Polish-Soviet border
Treaty of Sèvres was rejected by Turkish nationalists and superseded by the Treaty of Lausanne
San Remo decisions on Palestine mandate directly fueled Arab opposition expressed in the Nebi Musa riots
Gandhi's Non-Cooperation Movement was triggered partly by outrage over the 1919 Jallianwala Bagh massacre
The Kapp Putsch's failure and its aftermath revealed Weimar's vulnerability to right-wing coups, a pattern repeated in Hitler's 1923 attempt
The Russian Civil War was a direct consequence of the Bolshevik seizure of power in October 1917
The Turkish Grand National Assembly was established in response to the pending Treaty of Sèvres and Allied occupation
Bloody Sunday escalated the Irish War of Independence, leading to the truce and Anglo-Irish Treaty of 1921
Soviet conquest of the Caucasus was part of the broader Bolshevik victory in the Russian Civil War
The pattern of racial violence in 1920 continued and escalated into the Tulsa Race Massacre of 1921
The 1920 election was the first in which women voted nationally following the 19th Amendment
The Baku Congress and Gandhi's Non-Cooperation Movement both represented anti-imperial mobilization in 1920, though from different ideological traditions

_meta.note: Produced via Claude Code CLI (Max subscription) before API migration
